sentences.text: So being that I was the youngest and theoretically in a lessened danger than, let's say, my brother, so I went to open the door.
So I went down, and I opened up the door.
And these two huge German officers-- not just SS soldiers, but these were SS officers-- came in.
And, you know, they were wearing those high hats, officer hats, not-- you know.
And right away, they asked me, in German, where my sisters were.
They assumed that my aunts, you know, were my sisters.
So I like started in a crying voice.
I said, I don't know where they are.
They-- they took them away.
Maybe you can-- maybe you know where they are, saying, you're the-- you're the guys there, you know, right.
And so they kept searching the house.
They went into all the rooms.
And then they asked me where-- where the cellar was.
And every home had a-- I shouldn't say every home, but homes had cellars.
And the purpose was for-- there was no refrigeration, so they kept like milk and, butter, and stuff.
So it was like a drop close cellar.
So I showed him where it was.
We opened it up, and there were some steps going down.
So one of them went down.
And there wasn't much to search there because it was a small area.
And he came back up and he asked me where the attic was.
So the-- the attic was right on the, you know, on the left side where I was standing in like a hallway.
And it was a drop ladder.
So pointed up there.
And we opened up the-- the drop ladder.
And then he said to me, is anybody up there?
And I said, no.
And so he drew out his revolver, which to me looked like a cannon.
And he cocked it, and put it to my forehead, and said, is anybody up there?
Because if we find somebody up there, you're kaput.
And I said, no.
And he climbed up.
One of them climbed up the steps.
The other one was standing downstairs.
And he had a flashlight.
Luckily, my aunts and my family were hiding in an-- in an attic which was like at the end of the main attic and was like boarded up a little-- closed off.
So he looked and he looked.
Actually, something-- there was a noise up there like something fell, which maybe scared him.
And-- and-- and then he decided that there was nothing there and came down.
And then he asked me where-- where the garden was.
They had a big garden in the back of the house.
So they-- they went out to the garden.
Now, going out to the garden, they were right underneath the part of the attic where everybody was.
And-- and-- and I was only hoping that somebody doesn't cough at that point.
Or some noise, or they don't look up and get suspicious, you know.
And in fact, one of them did look.
And they walked out into the garden.
And they spent maybe five minutes or so, or 10, searching the garden because everything was grown high already.
It was like summer.
And then I heard them coming back on the side of the house and like leaving-- leaving the area.
And when I heard them, you know, pass the house and leave, and my teeth started chattering then like-- like a machine gun.
And that's when I started shaking.
You know, I went back [? bed. ?]
So that was-- that was now sort of a second phase.
But now, during that phase, they still didn't take children.
But now they took men, women.
They went through the houses.
And the Ukrainians were the ones that did most of the work there.
They knew where to look and how to look.
Now, who was up in that attic?
Your aunts?
My three aunts-- my father's three sisters-- my grandmother, and my sister.
And my mother.
They continued staying there, yeah.
They were there already for a few days, and we used to send up food to them.
And they wouldn't come down.
So there was already a like-- a complete action of killing Jews, an organized-- organized action of killing Jews.
Except they still didn't take children.
It-- it wasn't a farm.
It was just a house--
--basically This whole thing took at least seven days, maybe more, maybe 10 days.
And we just stayed there and, you know, tried-- we made food and tried to-- we sent up food for them.
And the way it was done, which was dangerous a little, there was a little opening in that part of the attic that they would open up.
And we would send it up with a rope, actually, send up the food.
And they would close it up again.
And then when it was over, the-- they let-- they let it be known that from today on the-- an order came, they don't kill Jews anymore, and that they can.
So true or not, people started coming out and back to their homes, those that survived.
And it turned out they probably-- during that process, they probably killed about 700, 1,000 people.
And we went back to-- to our house.
And now it's back, what you would say, quote unquote, 'normal.' You live, you Know.
But it-- it-- it's one of those situations that you know your life is not yours anymore.
They can come in any day, and take you, and-- and kill you.
And it's a frustrating situation because there's nothing you can do about it.
You're sitting, surrounded practically, in a town that you cannot leave town.
You have a curfew at night.
And during the day, you cannot leave town.
And if you're caught outside of the town, you can-- you can be killed.
So you know already from what is happening, now suddenly a whole family has disappeared.
And being that they didn't kill children at that point, a lot of children were left orphans.
So now, the Judenrat and some other people started organizing places for those children to live.
There are some who went with relatives that they had.
And they organized some rooms, you know.
They brought some furniture together.
And-- and they were feeding those children.
Where Were you still getting your food from, your family?
Well, we were getting it, basically, by bartering still.
The farmers would come in and buy, you know, things and bring us.
And then there were a lot of farmers in the area that were friendly.
So you would still get potatoes and you would get-- although it was not officially sanctioned.
But they-- they didn't really go around enforcing, you know, trying to catch somebody, you know.
So being that we did not have a ghetto, at one point they were thinking of moving some of the Jews from areas that were outside of the immediate and push them into one area.
But for some reason, they-- they didn't do it.
Because we were a ghetto as it was, you know, concentrated.
Then what happened that sort of changed this situation, this status quo, as it was, that you remember?
Well, then things continued and people-- you know, Jews had to go to work.
And-- and-- and the abuses were difficult, you know.
They were abusing people and doing whatever they wanted.
The next thing we heard was after the summer.
It was like middle of summer, in the summer-- well, in May.
We got word that the town of [?
Kovel, ?] which was a big city next to ours-- in-- in May, that they were killing all the Jews there.
And-- and the description was horrible.
You were-- there were dead people in the streets and there were people hanging.
And one Polish woman came back from there.
She went to visit somebody.
And she was describing the situation as corpses in the street.
So we knew, you know.
We knew what was happening.
Oh, sure, sure.
You know, that was the topic of conversations.
Very little else-- sometimes you wanted to get distracted.
Wasn't much else.
So that was in May.
And-- and then, in August, our turn came.
And we sort of had some feedback.
Somebody was-- again, I don't know how people knew.
Sometimes we would know because the SS trucks and-- and-- and motorcycles would come back into town.
And in a small town, it was-- they couldn't just come in without being noticed.
So-- and we knew that during the summer before and the spring when they did it, that that's what happened.
They came in with the trucks, bringing in the SS hidden in trucks-- and-- and then.
And not completely hidden-- the back was open.
You could see-- and then the motorcycles, you know, with the machine guns.
So-- but it was also possible that some of the Ukrainians warned-- especially friendly-- people that they were friends with.
So we knew that things were going to happen.
And at that time, my mother found a hiding place for us in a basement underneath stores.
And so it was on a Sunday afternoon or evening we went to that hiding place.
And so I went there, and my mother, and my brother, and my sister.
And when we got there, it was-- the basement was large.
And there must have been between 50 and 75 people already in there, whole families with children.
And they brought some bedding, you know, and some food, and some whatever.
So we slept there that night.
Monday morning we heard shots outside.
And from a particular spot in the basement that was close to the sidewalk, we were able to see outside.
And we were looking out, and we saw a particular incident where this-- this woman and her mother lived in a-- in one of the stores.
Usually people didn't live in the stores.
Stores were closed up.
And they-- they got in there, and they started to drag them out.
And they probably were dragging the mother, and the daughter was like, you know, putting up a fight.
And they shot her on the sidewalk.
So that was the first, probably, casualty in town.
Because they-- they tried to avoid killing people in town.
They didn't want to have to worry about the blood and stuff.
So they tried to take them.
Through the hole, yeah.
My brother and I were-- were looking out.
And so we knew that they were already killing.
In other words, we knew something was going to happen.
And this was-- it started like Monday morning.
It probably started before that.
The way they operated, they probably surrounded the town.
It was already surrounded, and you couldn't get out.
And so this was the starting point.
We-- we lasted there a few days.
There was one woman there that had maybe five or six children.
And one little girl, the youngest, was a few years old, was crying a lot.
So that was, of course, that was dangerous because they'll catch everybody.
So the mother decided to take that little girl out and put her to sleep up in-- in-- in the room on top in a bed.
And when the little girl woke up, or somebody woke her up, there was a Ukrainian policeman there.
And she-- the little girl was crying, mama, mama.
And he was saying to her, you know, where-- like she was leading him to-- to the place.
And she remembered the entrance.
The entrance there was through to-- to a toilet.
And so she was leading him in that direction.
And he was-- It was underneath the toilet?
Yeah.
There was an opening.
Like it was designed so that it could be moved away.
And it was underneath.
So this policemen, you know, he-- he knew where she was leading him, but he couldn't find any connection, any opening, you know.
So it took, I think, more than an hour or two before they finally-- because this little girl went away and she kept coming back to that same area.
And they got suspicious.
And they were looking.
They knew there was a hiding place there already, because the girl was going there.
And eventually, they-- they moved that whole assembly.
And it moved, and they-- so they found it.
And one of them stuck his rifle into the basement.
I saw already the light, you know.
They opened up.
So I knew it was all over.
And he was yelling to-- for everybody to get out.
[UKRAINIAN], in Ukrainian.
Come on up.
And there was a ladder, you know, leading up to the opening.
And so people started going out.
My mother spotted an area that was like boarded up.
There was like used to be a window there.
In fact, it was that area where we were peeking out.
There was a deeper area.
So she-- she pulled off one of the boards, and told me to get in, and pushed me in there.
And things were happening so fast that I-- and the nails wouldn't hold.
There were two nails like holding it.
So I was holding it by the nails inside.
Then everybody-- then they came down and they-- they started searching.
My mother hid under some bedding.
And they came down with-- two of them with rifles and bayonets.
And they were looking for things and they were poking.
And in the process, they-- they hit my mother with a bayonet.
And she was yelling, no, no.
I'm-- I'm coming out.
And then they-- they took her away.
And then they were looking, basically, for valuables.
They were finding little things and putting in their pockets.
And at one point, they were standing right next to me.
And I was afraid, you know.
I stopped breathing.
You know, I was afraid they're going to hear it.
Because it was-- it was a basement, it was very quiet.
And they were lighting-- they were lighting matches first.
And then they found a candle, so they lit a candle.
And then, basically, they just wanted to find whatever valuables, you know, like something with watches and stuff, and shoved it in their pockets, and they-- and then they left.
When they left, I really-- I really didn't know what to do because if you go out, they-- they-- they catch you.
And if you stay here, they're going to get you because they eventually-- they come to take away the rest of the stuff that's left there, they-- you know.
Suddenly, I hear somebody coming down the ladder.
And then when he-- when he came down the ladder, I-- I recognized him.
He was one of the Jews from the Judenrat.
In fact, he was the head of the Judenrat.
So I-- I came out from that-- I opened that board and I came out.
And he was searching.
And I asked him what he's-- what are you searching for?
And he said he's looking for galoshes that he-- so he must have been in there also somehow and left before.
Because he's planning to go away to the woods.
And it's very muddy there.
And, you know, [? see-- ?]
I didn't understand, basically, his-- you know, because that was dangerous.
What he was doing was very dangerous.
But I have a feeling he was already mixed up a little bit.
His mind like wasn't straight.
Then I asked him where he's going.
He says he's going to go up to the attic in-- in that house.
So I asked him if I could go with him.
And like in a way he was saying no.
But I followed him.
And we went up to this attic.
Then we heard a door-- somebody walk into that area.
And he walked to the edge of the attic to look down who it was.
And it was a Ukrainian policeman there with a rifle.
And he told him to come down.
And he asked him if somebody else was up there.
He said, no.
And then the Ukrainian, he gave him over to somebody else.
And he climbed up to search.
And I managed to crawl in like at the end of the attic where it becomes very narrow.
And there was some junk there.
I crawled in there.
And he came up, and looked, and looked, and didn't see anybody.
And he left.
And then I heard on the-- on the street-- because this was right next to the street they were taking.
One of them was asking what to do with him.
He said, take him to the synagogue.
And the synagogue was the-- the gathering place of the Jews when they captured them.
And then from there, they would take them to be shot.
So now, again, I don't know really what to do.
But I remembered my mother talking about a hiding place in that Hebrew teacher's house.
And-- and that was like a few streets away.
So slow I went down and I looked out.
And it was like dead at that point.
It was very quiet.
There was nobody on the street.
So I decided to walk to that other place.
And I made it to that other house.
And when I came in, the house was already emptied out.
It looks like everything was robbed and.
Because that was the-- the-- the way things were happening.
The minute the Jews were out of the houses, the local Ukrainians and also Ukrainians coming in from villages came in.
And they were going through the houses and grabbing whatever clothes was left and some furniture.
Blank, probably.
There was no plan.
Because you're there, you are practically suspended.
And three's-- you don't know what to do.
So the-- the first thing is to try to hide someplace.
Sure.
Sure.
I-- I really didn't know if I could go out because I didn't know what was happening outside.
And so when I-- when I came into that house, it was empty.
And I didn't know where the hiding place was.
But I may have heard that they were making a room.
You know, what people used to do is like take-- isolate that little room or build a little-- a double wall, you know, and make that a hiding place.
So the-- one of the-- the daughter's name was Malka.
And I remembered that.
So I-- I was calling for her.
I called, Malka, Malka.
And nobody answered.
And then I called again, Malka.
And nobody answered.
And I was ready to leave.
And suddenly, I hear a voice somewhere on a corner saying, shh, shh, don't, don't.
And they opened up a little-- little opening in the corner of the wall.
And-- and I crawled over there and they actually almost dragged me in there.
And I remember when I got in there, I like fainted.
I got very-- so they put me in a bed.
And they-- they had some vodka there.
So they gave me-- put some vodka on my-- on my lips.
And so now, that was--
OK.
So there were-- there were the husband and wife, that teacher, the old man that got knocked down by the Germans that time.
He was there and his wife.
And they were like old.
And then the daughter, Malka, and-- and a son, Mendel.
And they were like not married.
And they were probably in their late 20s.
And then there were two young boys.
One of them lived upstairs in an apartment-- and it's-- no, I'm sorry, upstairs in-- in an apartment where the first basement was.
And the other one was like across the street from this house.
So the process there was that one of them was an older boy.
He would go out in the middle of the night and try to find some food.
Because there was-- there was practically no food by then.
So he would bring like a loaf of bread.
And they-- there was a garden outside with tomatoes.
So he would take some-- the red ones were gone-- like green tomatoes.
He would bring them in.
And they had a barrel with water, so we had water.
Now, there was a well outside like-- there was like a yard between a few houses with a-- one of those wells with a chain crank.
So in the middle of the night, we would go out when it was very quiet and, when we ran out of water, tried to get more water.
Except-- except that-- that-- that chain and that crank were so squeaky, we'd have a problem.
We'd have to do it very slowly and make sure that nobody hears us.
Then, one time the older boy went out and never came back.
Now either he tried to run away to the woods-- he didn't survive the war-- or he got captured.
Longer than I thought.
Because I was trying to figure out, and I-- at some point, I lost track of how long I was.
I did remember that on Yom Kippur we were there.
And I remember they were praying, everybody was praying.
And they gave me a-- a prayer book to pray.
And I remember that day we didn't eat because it was Yom Kippur, so nobody had to eat.
Because during the other days, everybody used to get a little piece of bread like the size of a-- you know, fist, like, you know.
And it was up to you.
You could eat it all at once.
You could eat part in the morning, part-- whatever you like.
So this was a day we didn't eat.
And I remember looking at that book, at the prayer book.
And I couldn't say the words.
I was just staring at it, basically.
So I know-- then when eventually, when I got to Lubon, and they-- over there they started killing October 1.
Well, did you--
And I was only there about seven days.
So I can backtrack.
And that tells me that we were there about a month.
Hiding in that place.
Oh, I-- I knew already everybody was killed.
Except my-- my brother still ran away.
He was already in the synagogue.
And he later shows up in Lubon when I get there.
Yeah, we could-- I could hear shots.
We-- we knew what was going on, that everybody was being killed.
They--
--he knew some-- he knew some people-- you know, Christian families, Ukrainians-- that-- that were friendly.
And so he would go there and they would give him-- give him bread.
See, a lot of people, especially the older-- probably the older people-- were not so hostile as the younger people who were nationalistic and tended to, you know, act.
And then, of course, the-- the lowest and-- and-- and worst element joins up with the Germans, you know, to do whatever they want to do.
I didn't leave.
There's a limit to-- you know, we-- we thought, you know it's going to go on forever.
It can't, because eventually somebody finds you and you run out of food where you can't get any food.
So what happened-- we were-- we were running out of food.
We didn't have any food.
So I volunteered to go out and get some food.
So I chose-- I figured on a Sunday morning when everybody goes to church.
They had clothing that they were selling like for farm boys.
So I dressed up in one of those outfits.
And I went out to a gardener that I was friendly with to get some-- get some food.
When I got there, she got very frightened, you know, because she-- she was Polish Catholic.
And the-- the workers that she had there at that point were Ukrainians.
And she was afraid that they-- they may tell.
So-- and her husband wasn't there yet.
He was in church.
So she put me up in an attic.
And she said, you'll wait till the evening when the workers leave and then-- then you can go back.
She gave me some apples, and pears, and some bread.
And I was getting very sort of worried.
Yeah.
